evidence_quotes.quote: The internal currency is convinient for hiding the real price of purchased item, making it harder to see how much money is actually being used.
There is also hidden gambling often disquised in video games as gift boxes, that will always supposedly give the customer in return of the set amount of money spent worth of in-game items.
These games are sold as products, but are supposedly services that the company selling them can take away at time of their choosing.

paragraphs.text: There are many problems in video game industry practices that I feel should be addressed. First and formost, a widespread practic in video game market places is that the game has internal currency that the consumers buy with real money to then use this internal currency on further purchases. This internal currency has multiple uses, most of which are harmful to the user. The internal currency is convinient for hiding the real price of purchased item, making it harder to see how much money is actually being used. It is also used to deny refunds, when customer uses internal currency on something they did not like, their refund request is easier to ignore, because they did not use "real money" to make the purchase, but the internal currency they bought with real money. Almost always the internal currency is sold as set amounts, which means that if I want to buy item x that costs 750 imaginary coins, but the imaginary coin is only sold in packs of 500, 1000 or 2000, which means that no matter how I decide to spend my money, I will be left with excess currency. This is done so that the customer will be more likely to make a further purchace, since they already have some of the money left and don't want it to go to waste. There is also hidden gambling often disquised in video games as gift boxes, that will always supposedly give the customer in return of the set amount of money spent worth of in-game items. In reality, there is also a incredibly rare item that everyone really wants from these boxes, which makes these again lootboxes (gambling) in disquise. Couple games that have this practice are Wargaming titles World of Tanks and World of Warships, both of which I have experience of. Another significant and relevant topic regarding video games, is the destruction of video games after the publisher/developer decides that it is no longer profitable for them to keep the game alive and functioning. The games often come with forced online connection, meaning that playing the bought game is no longer possible once the servers are closed. These games are sold as products, but are supposedly services that the company selling them can take away at time of their choosing. Recent notable example is Ubisoft's "The Crew" where the game was shut down and everyone that owned the game was unable to access even the parts of the game that should not need any online connection to function such as the single player campaign. There is already an EU citizen initiative that is requesting change into this practice called Stop Killing Games, which has already collected over 1,4 million signatures and is closing within couple days of me writing this. Also outside the scope of video games, the recently surfaced issue with payment processors being able to effectively sensor services and products sold on digital platforms is something that also requires requlation. The video game marketplace websites Steam and Itch.io were both recently forced to remove some questionable nsfw titles that still fit within legal boundaries. I find it highly problematic that payment processors are able to decide what is acceptable to sell at the threat of withdrawing their services to the marketplace/company.
